title: Mixed Precision Training
concept: Mixed precision uses 16-bit floats (FP16/BF16) for activations to save memory and speed up computation, using 32-bit floats for master weights to maintain accuracy.

exercises.prompt: If a standard float uses 32 bits of memory, how much memory does a 16-bit float use?
exercises.explain: Computer numbers are stored in bits. Standard precision (FP32) uses 32 bits, while half-precision (FP16) uses 16 bits, which takes up half the space.

cards.title: Train AI Twice as Fast
cards.body: Training massive AI models requires huge amounts of computer memory and time. What if you could cut your memory usage in half and speed up training, without losing your model's intelligence? Mixed precision makes this magic trick possible!

cards.title: What is Mixed Precision?
cards.body: Mixed Precision Training is a technique where a model performs most of its calculations using smaller, faster 16-bit numbers (FP16 or BF16), but keeps a master copy of the model's weights in precise 32-bit numbers (FP32) to prevent errors.

cards.title: The Grocery List Shorthand
cards.body: Imagine writing a grocery list. You write 'Apples' and 'Milk' in tiny, quick shorthand (FP16) on a small scrap of paper to save space. But when you balance your monthly bank account, you use a large, official ledger with precise pennies (FP32). Mixed precision does the same: it uses quick shorthand for fast calculations, but keeps the master copy exact.

cards.title: Scaling Up Image Generators
cards.body: When training a modern image generator, the GPU performs millions of matrix multiplications. By switching the activations (temporary calculations) to FP16, the model uses half the memory bandwidth. To prevent tiny updates from disappearing, the system copies these back into FP32 master weights.

cards.title: Myth: Lower Precision Means Dumb AI
cards.body: People often think that using 16-bit numbers makes the model half as smart. In reality, neural networks don't need extreme precision for every single step. By keeping the critical master weights in 32-bit, the model maintains its full final accuracy!

cards.title: The Precision Balance
cards.body: By splitting the workload between two different levels of detail, we get the best of both worlds: speed and accuracy.

right.text: Used for master weights to preserve tiny details and accuracy.

left.text: Used for fast temporary calculations and activations to save memory.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary benefit of using 16-bit floats instead of 32-bit floats for activations during training?

options.text: It reduces memory usage and speeds up computations.
It makes the model's weights completely random.
It removes the need for any training data.
It guarantees that training takes zero seconds.

exercises.explain: Using smaller 16-bit numbers (FP16/BF16) requires less data storage, which frees up GPU memory and allows data to move much faster.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Mixed precision training completely eliminates 32-bit floats, converting everything to 16-bit permanently.
exercises.explain: False. Mixed precision training keeps a precise 32-bit master copy of the weights to ensure the model maintains its accuracy.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing precision type used to store the master weights.
exercises.sentence: To keep calculations fast, we use 16-bit floats, but we keep the master weights in [blank] to protect accuracy.
exercises.explain: We use FP16 (or BF16) for quick calculations to save memory, but we must keep the master weights in FP32 to maintain high precision and prevent mathematical errors.

exercises.prompt: Which of these AI models is using Mixed Precision Training?

options.text: Model A: 'I perform all calculations and keep all weights in standard 32-bit float format.'
Model B: 'I use 16-bit floats for fast math, but update a 32-bit master copy to keep things accurate.'
Model C: 'I train in full 32-bit precision, then discard half of my parameters when I am finished.'

exercises.explain: AI Model B describes mixed precision training. Option A is standard single-precision training, and Option C is post-training quantization (not mixed training).

exercises.prompt: A team wants to train a giant language model faster without losing accuracy. How does mixed precision training achieve this?

options.text: It trains two identical copies of the model at the same time to see which is faster
It uses fast 16-bit math for calculations, but updates a precise 32-bit master copy of weights
It randomly drops half of the data to save space in memory
It converts all numbers to integers to avoid using decimals entirely

exercises.explain: Mixed precision training uses FP16 for the heavy lifting (activations and gradients) to speed up training, but keeps a master copy of the weights in FP32 so that tiny updates aren't lost or rounded to zero.
